text: The surgeon performs thorough aspiration of the murky fluid in all four quadrants, focusing on cleaning the right and left hypochondrium around the liver and spleen. A bruise is identified on the mesenteric border of the sigmoid colon, while the cecum and other sections of the colon appear distended but uninjured. The sigmoid perforation margins are even and well-vascularized, with the seromuscular layers appearing healthy. The bruise on the sigmoid is explored with automatic graspers, revealing a full-thickness injury and visible lumen with recognizable mucosa. The perforation measures less than one centimeter.

The surgeon performs a primary laparoscopic repair of the colonic tear using intracorporeal interrupted stitches with 3-0 Vicryl, taking large seromuscular bites. The margins of the tear are even and bleeding, facilitating the repair process. Care is taken to grasp the epiploic appendage without damaging the colonic wall.
The surgeon completes the second layer of the colonic repair using a running serosal suture with Forth Eurovicryl, ensuring a fast and secure closure of the serosal layer.

The surgeon performs a laparoscopic repair of a colonic iatrogenic perforation. The margins of the tear are even and bleeding, allowing for a primary repair using double layer V-acryl sutures. The procedure includes a thorough washout and drainage of the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon performs repeated cycles of suction and irrigation around all quadrants of the abdominal cavity. No additional injuries or bowel lesions are visible.
